text.title: Mob Programming Roles and Styles
text.summary: Driver, navigator, and facilitator rules; four mob styles with flows and best-fit scenarios.

rule.statement: Apply the strong-style rule: for any idea to go from a person's head to the computer, it must pass through someone else's hands — the navigator never types.
rule.rationale: Forces verbalization of intent, making assumptions explicit and catchable before they become code.

list.item: Driver: only person at keyboard; asks clarifying questions; does not make independent design decisions; rotates every 5–15 minutes.
Navigator(s): think at higher level; direct the driver verbally; discuss approach among themselves; one navigator speaks at a time.
Facilitator (optional, recommended for mobs of 5+): manages rotation timer, ensures participation, handles interruptions, keeps the mob on goal.

rule.statement: In TDD mob: navigator describes test intent, driver writes failing test, rotate, new navigator describes implementation, driver makes it pass, rotate, refactor together.
rule.rationale: Combines mob knowledge-sharing with TDD discipline; tests are clear because intent is explained verbally to the group.

section.title: Learning Mob
section.p: Expert explains concepts, everyone tries hands-on, questions encouraged. Slower rotation (15–20 min). Focus on understanding, not output. No pressure for delivery.

rule.statement: Use intention-based navigation: state intent ("validate the email format") rather than dictation ("type if not @ in email").
rule.rationale: Dictation bypasses the driver's understanding; intention-based navigation keeps the driver thinking.

rule.statement: Rotate every 5–15 minutes on a visible timer; no exceptions for beginners until a natural rhythm develops.
rule.rationale: Fixed rotation prevents keyboard-hogging and forces participation from all members including introverts.

rule.statement: Limit mob to 3–8 people; more than 8 causes dominant personalities to crowd out quieter members.
rule.rationale: Mob efficiency drops sharply beyond 8 because coordination overhead exceeds the collaboration benefit.

antipattern.description: Tourist mob: participants not engaged, looking at phones, not contributing.
antipattern.reason: Breaks the knowledge-sharing contract; use round-robin navigation and active facilitation.

antipattern.description: Expert dominance: one person controls all decisions, others' ideas dismissed.
antipattern.reason: Facilitator must enforce round-robin; use "yes, and" rule to build on all ideas.

antipattern.description: No retrospective: problems persist across sessions.
antipattern.reason: Schedule 15 minutes of retro after every mob session; improvements are cumulative.
